howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.step01Body: Fill out a quick 30-second form with what you lost and where. It's free.
howItWorks.step02Body: Our team contacts Lost112, police stations, subway operators, bus companies, and taxi services — all in Korean so you don't have to.
howItWorks.step03Body: We email you as soon as your item is located, usually within 24-48 hours.
howItWorks.step04Body: Pay ${amount} to get the exact location, contact info, and English pickup instructions. Need delivery? We handle that too.

testimonials.title: Trusted by travelers
testimonials.subtitle: Real stories from people who got their belongings back.
testimonials.sarahQuote: I left my passport at a restaurant in Myeongdong. They found it within 6 hours. Lifesaver!
testimonials.kenjiQuote: Lost my wallet on the KTX to Busan. I was already back in Japan when they located it and shipped it to me.
testimonials.emmaQuote: My phone fell out on the subway. I had no idea how to use Lost112 in Korean. They handled everything.
testimonials.lucasQuote: Found my camera bag that I left at Incheon Airport in under 24 hours. Worth every dollar.

aboutUs.title: Who we are
aboutUs.body: We're a Seoul-based team of bilingual Koreans who help foreigners navigate Korea's lost and found system. We speak fluent Korean and English, and we know exactly how to work with Lost112, police stations, transit authorities, and taxi companies. Since Korea's lost item recovery system is almost entirely in Korean, most foreigners give up before even trying. That's where we come in.

categories.title: About the Service
categories.description: What we do and how we work

questions.q: What does Lost & Found Korea actually do?
questions.a: We help international travelers recover items they've left behind in Korea — bags, wallets, passports, electronics, anything you might have lost at an airport, hotel, locker, public transit station, or other Korean facility. We coordinate the search in Korean on your behalf, retrieve the item once located, package it carefully, and ship it to wherever you are in the world.

questions.q: Why do I need a service like this?
questions.a: Korean facilities are excellent at storing lost items, but they generally aren't equipped to handle international shipping, English correspondence, or follow-ups across time zones. Once you've left the country, the practical path back to your item often closes. We bridge that gap.

questions.q: Where in Korea do you operate?
questions.a: We work nationwide. We coordinate with airports, hotels, train and subway systems, taxi companies, and various other facilities across Korea. Some locations outside Seoul/Gyeonggi may carry a small surcharge.

questions.q: Are you affiliated with the Korean government or any airport?
questions.a: No. We're an independent, registered business in Korea. We work as your authorized agent — communicating with facilities and authorities on your behalf — but we are not part of any government agency or facility.

categories.title: Pricing & Payment
categories.description: Fees, currencies, and how to pay

questions.q: How much does it cost?
questions.a:
We offer three service tiers:

• **Recovery** — FREE to start, $39 if found. We search for free. You only pay $39 if we successfully locate your item and you wish to receive its location and pickup details.
• **All-in-One** — $79. A single-price service combining search, pickup coordination, and shipping.
• **Delivery Only** — $49. For when you already know where the item is being held; we handle pickup and shipping.

Surcharges may apply for locations outside the Seoul/Gyeonggi region (+$20).

questions.q: Are there other costs beyond the service fee?
questions.a: Yes. Service fees do not include third-party costs such as locker storage fees, international shipping, packaging materials, optional insurance, or customs duties. We provide an itemized estimate before you commit, so you'll know the full total in advance.

questions.q: How do I pay?
questions.a: We currently accept payment via PayPal (which also supports major credit cards). For custom invoices that include third-party costs (storage, shipping, etc.), we send you an itemized invoice that you can pay directly through PayPal.

questions.q: What currencies do you accept?
questions.a: Our fees are quoted in USD. Payment is processed in USD via PayPal, which automatically handles currency conversion at PayPal's exchange rate if you pay from a different currency.

questions.q: Why do I have to pay before you ship?
questions.a: Once we begin pickup, packaging, and shipping coordination, work and third-party costs are immediately incurred on your behalf — locker fees, packaging materials, postage. Prepayment is what allows us to handle those costs and ship without delay.

questions.q: How do I report a lost item?
questions.a:
Submit a brief report through our website. Include:

• A description of the item
• Where you think you lost it (or last saw it)
• Approximate date and time of loss
• Your contact information and current location

The initial report is free. You only commit to a service fee once we either locate your item (Recovery tier) or you confirm a service (All-in-One / Delivery Only).

questions.q: How long does recovery typically take?
questions.a: It varies depending on where the item is held and how quickly the facility responds. Most cases are located within 24–72 hours. Some take longer, particularly with smaller facilities or items lost in transit. We'll keep you updated throughout.

questions.q: What if you can't find my item?
questions.a: For the Recovery tier, there's no charge if we can't locate your item. For All-in-One service, if we cannot locate the item before pickup begins, we will refund the LFK service fee.

questions.q: Will you open my bag?
questions.a: Only if you ask us to, or if a facility/customs requires it. If you prefer the bag stay sealed, we honor that completely — we'll repackage it in a protective outer box without opening the original. Just let us know your preference.

questions.q: How will my item be shipped?
questions.a: We typically use EMS (Korea Post's international express service) or EMS Premium, which are reliable, trackable, and well-suited for international delivery. Express options (DHL, FedEx, UPS) are available if you need faster delivery, though they cost significantly more. We'll recommend an option based on your item's size, weight, and urgency.

questions.q: Do you offer tracking?
questions.a: Yes. Every shipment includes a tracking number that you can use on Korea Post's site, USPS (for U.S. shipments), or unified tracking sites like 17track.net.

questions.q: How long does shipping take?
questions.a:
• **EMS Standard:** typically 7–14 days
• **EMS Premium:** typically 5–7 days
• **Express (DHL/FedEx/UPS):** typically 3–5 days

Times vary by destination country and customs processing.

questions.q: Is shipping insured?
questions.a: Yes. EMS includes basic insurance, and we add declared-value insurance up to your stated item value at no extra cost to you, for protection during international transit. For high-value items, additional carrier insurance is available.

questions.q: What if my item is lost or damaged in shipping?
questions.a: Once handed off to the carrier, the package is in their custody and shipping risk transfers to them. If something goes wrong in transit, the carrier or insurance provider handles the claim. We provide all documentation needed to support your claim and assist throughout the process.

questions.q: Can I get a refund?
questions.a: Our services are prepay-and-go. Once payment is made, we begin coordination and incur third-party costs immediately on your behalf, so payments are generally non-refundable. The exception is if we cancel a service ourselves (e.g. the item cannot legally be shipped) — in which case we refund LFK service fees minus any third-party costs already incurred. See our Refund Policy for full details.

questions.q: What about customs and duties?
questions.a: You're responsible for any customs duties or import taxes imposed by your country. We declare item values honestly on customs forms based on the value you provide. For shipments under most countries' de minimis thresholds (e.g. $800 for the U.S.), no duties typically apply.

questions.q: Can you ship items I'm not sure are legal to mail?
questions.a: We don't ship anything illegal, hazardous, or restricted under Korean export law or your country's import law. This includes weapons, certain medications, ivory, and other regulated items. If we're unsure about a specific item, we'll consult with the post office before proceeding.

questions.q: How is my personal information handled?
questions.a: We only collect what we need to coordinate the recovery and shipping. We never sell your data, and we share information only with the facilities and carriers necessary to complete your service. See our Privacy Policy for details.

categories.title: Trust & Verification
categories.description: Why you can rely on us

questions.q: How do I know this isn't a scam?
questions.a: Fair question. We're a registered business in Korea (registration number visible in our footer and legal pages). We provide tracking numbers, photographs of the packaging process, official post office receipts, and continuous updates. Payment is processed through PayPal, which provides its own buyer protection. We're happy to answer any questions before you commit.

questions.q: Will I get receipts and documentation?
questions.a:
Yes. We provide:

• A clear estimate before payment
• A PayPal payment receipt
• Photographs of the packaging process
• The official shipping receipt and tracking number
• Customs declaration documentation

questions.q: What if I have a problem during the process?
questions.a: Email us at [support@lostandfoundkorea.com](mailto:support@lostandfoundkorea.com). We respond promptly. If something goes wrong, we'll work with you to resolve it transparently.

faq.subtitle: Everything we get asked about how Lost & Found Korea works. Search or browse by category.
faq.noResultsBody: No questions match your search. Try different keywords.

tabs.label: What we saw
paragraphs: Anyone who spends time around hotels or airports eventually notices a quiet scene that repeats itself: someone's suitcase, someone's wallet, someone's passport sitting in a corner behind the counter, waiting for days, weeks, sometimes months.
For a long time, I walked past these things without thinking much about them. But over time, those objects started to look different to me.

Some of the bags were clearly expensive — items someone had hesitated over for a long time before finally purchasing, things they had taken care of carefully. Some of the wallets contained credit cards alongside a small photograph of a child. Some of the laptops likely held the entirety of someone's work and personal life.

But what weighed on me most were the items whose value couldn't be measured in money. Old journals. Small boxes holding handwritten letters. Cameras filled with photos from an entire trip. Wristwatches that looked as though they'd been passed down from a parent or grandparent. Things that couldn't be replaced. Things that held time that couldn't be remade.

Every time I noticed those items still sitting in the same spot weeks or months later, something in me felt heavy. Someone, somewhere, was probably still looking for that thing. Or had already given up and was learning to live with the empty space where it used to be.
That's where the question started. Why are these items not making it back to their owners? The facilities are clearly storing them well — so what is missing?

tabs.label: Why items don't make it home
paragraphs: Following the question led to an answer, and the answer turned out to be no one's fault.

Hotels, airports, airlines, transit systems — these institutions take their responsibility for lost items seriously. In Korea especially, they take it remarkably seriously. Items are stored well. Records are kept carefully. Up to that point, the system is excellent.
The problem begins at the next step: actually returning the item to its owner.

That part of the work is, by its nature, almost entirely human. Someone has to call the facility in Korean. Someone has to communicate with a customer in another time zone, in their language. Someone has to confirm exactly how the customer wants the item handled — opened or sealed, packaged how, insured for what value. Someone has to coordinate international shipping. Someone has to prepare customs paperwork. Someone has to follow up after the item is sent. Each case is its own small project.

Here is where the structural problem becomes visible: the more actively a facility tries to do this work, the deeper the loss they take on. Storage space is occupied. Staff time is consumed. Multilingual support is needed. Resources have to be invested in international shipping and customs work that has nothing to do with their core business. All of it costs money.

And who covers that cost? No one. There is no revenue in this work. Facilities don't have a way to make money from returning lost items. The harder they try, the more they lose, with no way to recover any of it.

If this work could generate revenue, airlines and hotels and government agencies would have built better systems for it long ago. But it can't. It's a cost center, not a business line. So every facility, very rationally, stops halfway. They store the item. They keep the record. But the final step — getting the item back to its owner — is no one's job.

This isn't a Korea-specific problem. The same is true in Japan, in Germany, in the United States. Lost-and-found has always been a "cost to be tolerated" rather than "an investment to be made." The economics never worked anywhere.

tabs.label: What changed
paragraphs: The work of returning a lost item to its owner is, in its core, almost entirely human work — work of language and judgment.

Politely persuading a hotel staffer to check the lost-and-found bin one more time. Translating a customer's anxious description into Korean polite enough that a Korean staff member will actually pull the records. The endless back-and-forth with a customer in another time zone about whether to open the bag, how to package it, which insurance to add. Knowing when to push a facility for a faster response. Knowing when to back off.
Each case is its own negotiation, its own piece of correspondence, its own chain of small decisions. None of it can be automated.

To run this as a business meant hiring multilingual staff, training them in Korean institutional culture, paying their salaries, and somehow making the math work on small service fees. The labor costs eat the margin alive. That's why no one had built it. Not because the gap was invisible. Because the unit economics didn't work.
The leap in AI tools over the last year or two changed that math.

We don't want to be misunderstood. AI is not replacing human judgment here. The careful work of deciding whether to open a customer's bag, how to phrase a difficult conversation, when to push a facility, when to hold back — that remains entirely human. It has to be.

But the work surrounding that judgment — accurate translation between two languages, drafting bilingual correspondence, organizing customs paperwork, building and maintaining the technical systems that hold this all together, organizing and searching case histories — all of that is now within reach for a small team with the right tools. Work that would have required five or six people five years ago can now be handled by a far smaller group.
That changed the unit economics. Labor stopped eating the entire margin. Suddenly this work could exist as a real business and not just as goodwill or a side project.

tabs.label: How LFK started
paragraphs: So we started.

Lost & Found Korea focuses on one thing: returning items lost in Korea to their owners abroad. We don't try to do everything. We don't try to replace the careful work the facility staff already do. We simply handle the part the facility was never resourced to handle — the final mile.

We contact the facility in Korean. We retrieve the item ourselves. We package it carefully. We follow the customer's wishes exactly — if they ask us not to open the bag, we don't. If they want insurance, we add it. We ship it to wherever they are in the world. We update them in their language at every step.

We charge a clear, transparent fee. Third-party costs are disclosed in advance. There are no hidden charges. Because we're a registered business in Korea, we can offer something that informal helpers cannot: accountability. Insurance, tracking, a clear refund policy, the kind of standing-behind-the-work that gives a stranger in another country a reason to trust us with something they care about.
We're a small operation. We keep it small on purpose. Our focus is deep, not wide: international travelers who have lost something in Korea, and the careful work of getting that something home.

tabs.label: Our vision
paragraphs: There is a gap in the world. A gap that isn't anyone's fault — a gap that exists for structural reasons. Facilities handle storage. Governments fulfill their share of responsibility. But the final step — returning the item to its owner — has always been left empty.
Lost & Found Korea exists to close that gap.

Our vision goes beyond Korea. We believe that meaningful belongings — the ones carrying memories, history, and parts of someone's life — should not be wasted, forgotten, or thrown away simply because the system wasn't built to send them home. They deserve to find their way back to the people they belong to.

We're starting in Korea, but the same structural gap exists almost everywhere. Facilities store. Governments oversee. But returning items to their owners has been no one's job, anywhere. Someday, we hope to close that gap far beyond Korea — so that no matter where in the world an item is lost, there is a trustworthy way home for it.
If you're reading this, having lost something in Korea while being somewhere else in the world, we just want you to know: there's a way to get it home. The system has a gap. We're here to close it.

shippingGuide.title: Fast EMS shipping to Japan
shippingGuide.subtitle: Korea Post EMS reaches most of Japan in 2–3 business days, with full tracking and a clear customs trail. Here's what to expect.
shippingGuide.point1Body: EMS Premium from Seoul to Tokyo / Osaka / Fukuoka typically lands in 2–3 business days. Standard EMS adds 1–2 days.
shippingGuide.point2Body: Korea Post → Japan Post tracking is unified. You see every scan from pickup to your door.
shippingGuide.point3Body: We declare the item value honestly on the customs form. Personal items under the duty-free threshold usually clear without paperwork on your end.
shippingGuide.point4Body: EMS includes basic loss/damage coverage. We can add declared-value insurance on request — no extra charge from us.

lostFoundExplainer.title: How Korean lost & found actually works
lostFoundExplainer.paragraph1: Korea's lost-and-found system is excellent at the storage step. Police, transit, and hotels log items diligently, and the national Lost112 database is genuinely comprehensive — but it's all in Korean, and the procedures vary by venue.
lostFoundExplainer.paragraph2: For a Japanese traveler back home, that means: phone calls in Korean, no English (let alone Japanese) staff at smaller venues, and shipping logistics nobody at the venue is responsible for handling.
lostFoundExplainer.paragraph3: We close that gap. We make the calls in Korean, confirm with you in your language, retrieve the item, package it, and put it on EMS to your address. Each case stays with the same handler from start to finish.

shippingMap.title: We ship to wherever home is
shippingMap.subtitle: Items recovered in Korea, delivered to over 30 countries. EMS, EMS Premium, and express options (DHL/FedEx/UPS) available based on your speed and budget.
shippingMap.region1Example: USA, Canada — typically 5–7 business days via EMS
shippingMap.region2Example: UK, Germany, France — typically 6–10 business days
shippingMap.region3Example: Australia, New Zealand — typically 5–8 business days
shippingMap.region4Example: Japan, Singapore, Taiwan — typically 2–4 business days

supportTrust.title: No translation barrier. No surprise fees.
supportTrust.point1Body: Every email, every status update, every customs question — answered in clear English by the same handler your case is assigned to.
supportTrust.point2Title: Korean is our problem, not yours
supportTrust.point2Body: We make the calls, send the polite Korean follow-ups, and translate the venue's instructions for you. You never need to learn Lost112's interface.
supportTrust.point3Body: Pay directly in USD via PayPal. No FX guesswork, no hidden conversion fees, full PayPal buyer protection.
